text: The surgeon uses cold scissors to dissect and separate the small bowel adhered to the right adnexa and the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon begins the planned medial to lateral dissection, ensuring the ureter is visible before proceeding to take the vessel. Indocyanine green (ICG) in the stents is used for enhanced visualization.

The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) just proximal to the takeoff of the left colic artery. A tunnel is created, and the vessel sealer is used to divide the vessel after tension is relieved. The dissection continues from the medial space until visibility is limited, prompting the placement of a sponge before transitioning to the lateral dissection.

The surgeon enters the medially dissected plane, revealing the sponge placed earlier. The mesentery is lifted off the kidney as the dissection progresses towards the splenic flexure. The vessel sealer is used to navigate the plane between the splenic flexure and the left kidney.

The patient remains in steep Trendelenburg position with a slight left side elevation. The surgeon has a clear view of the surgical field and successfully mobilizes the splenic flexure from an inferior lateral approach. The inferior border of the pancreas is visible as the surgeon continues mobilization along its base.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection to separate diseased tissue adhered to the patient's uterus, carefully detaching it from the left pelvic sidewall. The dissection continues down from the dome of the bladder and the uterus, revealing a small granulation-filled cavity.
The surgeon has freed the colon and is now clearing the rectal margin of its mesentery, followed by clearing the proximal margin in preparation for transrectal extraction of the specimen.

The surgeon approaches the valve wall, marking the proximal and distal areas with cautery to check perfusion. The upper rectum is divided using scissors, chosen over the vessel sealer to minimize thermal spread.

The surgeon uses a ring clamp to remove a wad of mesentery and proceeds to chop the specimen into smaller pieces for extraction. The process involves repeatedly going below to retrieve each piece individually, using scissors when the vessel sealer is insufficient for cutting through dense tissue.
The surgeon removes the final two pieces of the diseased colon, ensuring each transected piece is extracted individually to prevent any from flipping into the upper abdomen.

The surgeon performs a sewn two-layer anastomosis on the transected bowel wall, starting with the back outer row of seromuscular bites a few millimeters from the edge. The procedure involves using 3-0 nine-inch V-Lux sutures for the outer row and potentially six-inch sutures for the inner row, beginning in the middle of the anastomosis.

The surgeon continues the anastomosis by sewing the inner back row, starting in the middle and moving towards the near corner. The stitch is turned around the far corner, transitioning to the inner anterior row using a canal suture to imbricate the lip. The surgeon terminates the far suture due to unfavorable angles and completes the stitch with the nearsight suture.
The surgeon ties the two suture rows together and sews the original posterior outer row around anteriorly. A leak test is performed to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a 19mm rigid scope to inspect the anastomosis, confirming the absence of bubbles and bleeding, indicating a successful seal. The surgical area is then cleaned up, marking the completion of the procedure.
